other_school_based_wellness_activities.o.answer:
Our district has an additional wellness goal focused on social-emotional health and its connection to nutrition and physical activity. Specifically, we are working to integrate mindfulness, stress-management, and body-positive education into our broader wellness efforts.

For example, at Maple Grove Middle School we have begun piloting a "Mindful Mornings" program twice a week, where homeroom teachers lead a short, structured routine that includes a breathing exercise and a brief discussion about topics like fueling your body for learning, recognizing hunger and fullness cues, and managing the anxiety that some students feel around weight and body image. This is being done in collaboration with our school counselor and our district wellness committee, which includes parents, a pediatrician from the local clinic, and our food service director.

We are also developing age-appropriate lessons that help students critically evaluate media messages related to food, dieting, and body size. The goal is to reduce stigma, discourage unhealthy dieting behaviors, and promote a more balanced view of health that includes sleep, movement, mental health, and relationships, not just weight. These lessons are being piloted in 6th and 8th grade health classes this year, with the intent to expand to all grades over the next two years.

Additionally, we are working on creating "calm corners" in the cafeteria for younger students who may feel overwhelmed by noise and crowds. These areas are simply quieter tables where students can sit with a friend or paraprofessional and still participate fully in school meals. The intent is to ensure that students with sensory needs or anxiety still get adequate nutrition and a positive eating experience, rather than rushing or skipping meals.

other_school_based_wellness_activities.n.answer:
Our district wellness team, led by Wellness Coordinator Sarah Martinez, meets quarterly to review progress on all school-based wellness activities and to identify gaps between written policy and actual practice in buildings.

Free drinking water: All cafeterias have at least one chilled water bottle-filling station in addition to traditional fountains. In 2023–2024, we used a small grant from the county health department to purchase reusable water bottles for all 6th graders to encourage hydration. Custodial staff have a checklist to ensure fountains and stations are cleaned and functioning daily.

Nutrition staff qualifications and training: All of our kitchen managers hold at least a ServSafe certification, and new hires receive on-the-job training that covers safe food handling, allergy awareness, and an overview of the district wellness policy. We track the required USDA professional standards hours using an online learning management system, and we schedule in-person refreshers on topics like sodium reduction, scratch cooking, and accommodating special diets.

Meal environment and time to eat: Elementary students receive a minimum of 20 minutes of seated time for lunch, not counting travel to and from the cafeteria. After feedback from parents and students, the middle school adjusted its bell schedule this year to avoid having lunch periods start before 10:30 a.m. or after 1:00 p.m. We also moved recess to occur before lunch at two of our three elementary schools, which teachers report has reduced food waste and improved student behavior during the afternoon.

Alternate breakfast models: To address low breakfast participation at the middle school, we added a grab-and-go breakfast cart near the main entrance that stays open through the first passing period. At the elementary level, we piloted "Breakfast in the Classroom" for 3rd grade, where students can eat a simple breakfast at their desks during morning announcements. Participation has increased, especially among students who arrive right at the bell or slightly late.

Handwashing and sanitizing: All schools have hand sanitizer dispensers at the cafeteria entrance, and teachers are encouraged to build in a brief handwashing routine before lunch, especially in K–3 classrooms. During cold and flu season, the school nurse sends reminders to staff about proper handwashing, and we share short videos with students during morning announcements.

Parent communication and role modeling: We send a monthly digital "Wellness Update" through the district newsletter that includes the menu highlights, a simple, low-cost recipe that kids can help prepare, and tips for staying active as a family (such as local free events or ideas for indoor movement during winter). We also invite parents and guardians to join students for lunch on designated "Family Lunch Days," which has helped adults see the quality of school meals and model trying new fruits and vegetables.

Fundraising and outside funding: The district has shifted away from candy-based fundraisers and now encourages walk-a-thons, fun runs, and sales of non-food items (e.g., school-branded water bottles or spirit wear). The wellness committee reviews fundraising proposals that involve food to ensure they align with Smart Snacks standards. We actively pursue grants from state and local agencies and from non-profits to support garden projects, staff wellness challenges, and additional physical activity equipment.

Overall, while most of the listed wellness activities are fully in place, the committee has identified a need for more consistent implementation across all buildings, especially regarding staff training frequency and the depth of social-emotional wellness integration. Our next step is to create a simple building-level checklist that principals can use annually to self-assess and plan improvements.
